claims.claim_text: The opening fintech outage anecdote presents a specific December 2021 incident with precise service counts, cause chain, and outage duration.
claims.anchor_text: In December 2021, a Series C fintech startup running 340 microservices on Amazon EKS experienced a cascading failure that took down their entire payment processing platform for nearly four hours. The root cause was not a bug in their code. It was not a misconfigured Kubernetes manifest. The engineering team had deployed their EKS cluster with a public API server endpoint and no private endpoint. When a regional AWS API throttling event occurred, their worker nodes -- running in private subnets -- could not reach the Kubernetes API server through the NAT Gateway because the NAT Gateway itself was overwhelmed by unrelated outbound traffic from a data pipeline that had gone haywire. Nodes lost contact with the control plane, pods were evicted, and the entire fleet entered a crash loop. A private endpoint configuration would have kept node-to-control-plane traffic entirely within the VPC, completely bypassing the NAT Gateway and the public internet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A cluster that relies only on the public API endpoint can create an unnecessary dependency on NAT or other external network paths for node-to-control-plane communication. Enabling the private endpoint keeps that traffic inside the VPC and reduces that failure mode.
claims.rationale: This is a highly specific outage story with no attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The EKS control plane uses at least two API server instances and three etcd instances across three Availability Zones.
claims.span_hint: section: EKS Control Plane Architecture / What AWS Manages For You
claims.rationale: AWS documents this exact control-plane layout in the EKS architecture guide.

claims.claim_text: The standard-support EKS cluster fee is $0.10 per cluster-hour.
claims.span_hint: section: EKS Control Plane Architecture / What AWS Manages For You
claims.rationale: AWS pricing currently lists standard Kubernetes version support at $0.10 per cluster per hour.

claims.claim_text: When you create a cluster, EKS creates 2-4 network interfaces in the specified subnets and uses them for cluster-to-VPC and kubelet API communication.
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly documents the 2-4 interfaces and their communication role.

claims.claim_text: EKS automatically associates the cluster security group with the control-plane ENIs and with managed node group node interfaces, and its default rules allow unrestricted cluster-to-node traffic.
claims.rationale: The EKS security group requirements page states these associations and default rules directly.

claims.claim_text: A new EKS cluster is public-endpoint by default, and node-to-control-plane requests in that mode leave the VPC.
claims.rationale: AWS documents public endpoint access as the default and describes the public-only traffic path.

claims.claim_text: The module says public-only worker-node traffic traverses 'the public internet' to reach the API server.
claims.anchor_text: Your worker nodes in private subnets must traverse the NAT Gateway and the public internet to reach the API server.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Your worker nodes in private subnets must reach the API server through the public endpoint, which sends that traffic out of the VPC.
claims.rationale: AWS says public-only traffic leaves the VPC but not Amazon's network, so 'public internet' is overstated.

claims.claim_text: With a private-only endpoint, kubectl access must come from within the VPC or a connected network, and there is no public internet access to the API server.
claims.rationale: The cluster endpoint guide states these private-only access constraints explicitly.

claims.claim_text: AWS best practices recommend enabling both public and private endpoint access and restricting public endpoint CIDRs when internet access is needed.
claims.rationale: The EKS subnet best-practices guide recommends public-and-private mode with restricted public CIDRs.

claims.claim_text: Managed node groups automate node provisioning and lifecycle, and managed updates drain nodes while respecting PodDisruptionBudgets.
claims.rationale: AWS documents automated lifecycle management, draining behavior, and PDB handling for managed node groups.

claims.claim_text: The module says multiple instance types in a managed node group make EKS use the cheapest available capacity.
claims.span_hint: section: Managed Node Groups / Key features of MNGs
claims.anchor_text: Specify a list and MNGs will use the cheapest available (critical for Spot)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Specify multiple instance types to widen available capacity pools; On-Demand groups use the order you provide, and Spot groups use AWS allocation strategies such as price-capacity-optimized.
claims.rationale: AWS documents prioritized ordering for On-Demand and allocation strategies for Spot, not a simple 'cheapest available' rule.

claims.claim_text: EKS Fargate gives each pod its own isolated VM boundary and does not support DaemonSets, privileged containers, or GPUs.
claims.rationale: AWS documents per-pod VM isolation and these specific Fargate limitations.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a specific 30-90 second Fargate cold-start penalty versus node-based scheduling.
claims.anchor_text: Pods take 30-90 seconds longer to start compared to node-based scheduling
claims.suggested_rewrite: Pods on Fargate generally take noticeably longer to become ready than pods scheduled onto already-running EC2 nodes
claims.rationale: AWS documents the different scheduling model and isolation boundary, but not this exact startup-time range.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a specific 20-40% Fargate cost premium over equivalent EC2 for sustained workloads.
claims.anchor_text: Typically 20-40% more expensive than equivalent EC2 for sustained workloads, but eliminates operational overhead
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cost depends on workload shape and operational trade-offs; Fargate can reduce node-management overhead but is not universally the cheapest option
claims.rationale: The percentage is an uncited cost benchmark and not a stable vendor fact.

claims.claim_text: EKS does not automatically update add-ons when new versions are released or after a cluster minor-version upgrade, so compatibility must be checked and updates initiated explicitly.
claims.span_hint: section: EKS Add-ons: Managed Component Lifecycle
claims.rationale: AWS says add-ons are not auto-updated and that compatibility should be verified before updating.

claims.claim_text: Using PRESERVE keeps existing custom add-on values, while not using it lets EKS overwrite them with defaults.
claims.rationale: The EKS add-on update docs describe the exact conflict-resolution behavior for PRESERVE versus overwrite.

claims.claim_text: EKS automatically installs self-managed VPC CNI, kube-proxy, and CoreDNS for every cluster, while other AWS add-ons are optional.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the default self-managed add-ons and the broader curated add-on catalog separately.

claims.claim_text: The aws-auth ConfigMap is deprecated, and the cluster creator IAM principal is automatically granted hidden system:masters access.
claims.span_hint: section: The Legacy System: aws-auth ConfigMap
claims.rationale: The aws-auth documentation states both the deprecation and the hidden cluster-creator access behavior.

claims.claim_text: EKS access management controls were introduced on December 18, 2023 and let administrators manage IAM-to-cluster access through EKS APIs.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern System: EKS Access Entries
claims.rationale: AWS's launch announcement gives the date and describes the API-based access-management feature.

claims.claim_text: EKS provides predefined access policies named AmazonEKSClusterAdminPolicy, AmazonEKSAdminPolicy, AmazonEKSEditPolicy, and AmazonEKSViewPolicy.
claims.rationale: The access-policies guide lists these predefined EKS access-policy names.

claims.claim_text: In API_AND_CONFIG_MAP mode, access entries take precedence over matching aws-auth ConfigMap mappings for the same IAM principal.
claims.span_hint: section: Migration Path: aws-auth to Access Entries
claims.rationale: AWS states this precedence rule explicitly in the migration guide.

claims.claim_text: Once you switch authentication mode to use the EKS API, you cannot revert to a mode that removes access entries and the EKS API.
claims.span_hint: section: Migration Path: aws-auth to Access Entries
claims.rationale: AWS documents this one-way authentication-mode restriction directly.

claims.claim_text: Enabling the private endpoint makes EKS create a Route 53 private hosted zone associated with the VPC, and the endpoint resolves to private IPs from the VPC side.
claims.rationale: The cluster endpoint guide describes the private hosted zone and private-IP resolution behavior.

claims.claim_text: The module says AWS recommends at least /24 subnets with 251 usable IPs for EKS.
claims.anchor_text: AWS recommends subnets with at least a `/24` (251 usable IPs) for EKS.
claims.suggested_rewrite: AWS requires each cluster subnet to have at least six free IP addresses and recommends at least 16; plan larger subnets when your node and Pod density require them.
claims.rationale: Current AWS guidance does not support this /24 recommendation and instead gives smaller minimums for cluster subnets.

claims.claim_text: The module says aws-auth was a temporary 2018 solution, took over five years to replace, and drove thousands of AWS Support recovery tickets per quarter.
claims.anchor_text: The original `aws-auth` ConfigMap was created as a "temporary" solution in 2018 when EKS launched. It took AWS over five years to replace it with Access Entries. During those five years, the `aws-auth` ConfigMap became the single most common cause of EKS cluster lockouts, with AWS Support handling thousands of recovery tickets per quarter.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The `aws-auth` ConfigMap shipped with early EKS releases and remained the primary IAM-to-RBAC integration mechanism until AWS introduced access entries in December 2023.
claims.rationale: The support-ticket volume and 'temporary solution' framing are not backed by a cited primary source here.

claims.claim_text: The EKS Pod Identity agent runs on each node, and Pod Identity avoids configuring a separate OIDC identity provider per cluster.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes / Not setting up the EKS Pod Identity agent
claims.rationale: AWS best-practices documentation describes Pod Identity as an agent-based feature that removes per-cluster OIDC setup.

claims.claim_text: The aws-auth lockout anecdote presents a specific six-hour outage and recovery sequence without attribution.
claims.span_hint: section: The Legacy System: aws-auth ConfigMap
claims.anchor_text: *War Story: A platform team lost cluster access for six hours after a junior engineer ran `kubectl edit configmap aws-auth -n kube-system` and accidentally deleted the `mapRoles` section entirely. All nodes lost their RBAC bindings and began reporting `Unauthorized`. The only recovery path was to use the cluster creator's IAM credentials (which had been rotated months ago) to restore the ConfigMap. They implemented GitOps for the aws-auth ConfigMap the next day, but the real fix was migrating to Access Entries.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: Directly editing `aws-auth` is risky: a bad change can break IAM-to-RBAC mappings and leave teams scrambling to recover access. Moving to access entries reduces that operational risk.
claims.rationale: This is another precise operational anecdote with no attributable source.

further_reading.title: Amazon EKS architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical AWS overview of EKS control-plane and compute architecture.

further_reading.title: Cluster API server endpoint
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the primary reference for public versus private endpoint behavior, DNS, and access paths.

further_reading.title: Migrating existing aws-auth ConfigMap entries to access entries
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers precedence, migration sequencing, and operational cautions for moving off aws-auth.

notes: Quiz items, quiz answers, code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, and hands-on exercise steps were excluded per the seed rules. Repeated claims that were purely restatements of already-captured facts were not duplicated unless the later section introduced new precision or a separate citation need.
